text.title: Technical Debt Framework
text.summary: Defines technical debt types, the deliberate/inadvertent quadrant, and the six-step process for making debt visible, quantified, and strategically paid down.

section.title: Debt Types
section.p: Technical debt is the implied cost of additional rework caused by choosing easy solutions now instead of better approaches that take longer — borrowing against future productivity.

list.item: Deliberate: conscious shortcut, e.g. "ship now, refactor later."
Accidental: discovered a better pattern after the fact.
Bit rot: environment changed around stable code — outdated dependencies.
Design debt: architecture that cannot scale to the next requirement.
Documentation debt: missing docs mean only one person can maintain the system.
Test debt: missing tests prevent safe changes to production code.

rule.statement: Deliberate prudent debt is acceptable; deliberate reckless debt is not. Inadvertent debt must be discovered and registered as soon as identified.
rule.rationale: Reckless debt accumulates compounding interest; prudent debt is a conscious trade-off with a planned repayment date.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Make debt visible: tag every known item in the issue tracker with a tech-debt label; capture what, why, time tax, and fix cost per item.
rule.rationale: Invisible debt cannot be prioritized; registration is the prerequisite for all downstream steps.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Quantify impact: measure interest as (development slowdown × frequency) + risk cost. Require at least one evidence source (build-time graph, incident post-mortem, PR cycle time delta).
rule.rationale: Without instrumented data the interest score is political; evidence anchors the number to reality.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Prioritize: score each item on interest rate (40%), contagion (20%), effort to fix (20%), alignment with planned work (20%). Priority = Impact × Alignment / Effort.
rule.rationale: Alignment-weighted scoring ensures the lowest marginal cost paydown happens during adjacent feature work.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Allocate capacity: reserve 15-20% of every sprint continuously plus one focused quarterly debt sprint. Maintain a separate, visible budget line — never let it merge with the feature backlog.
rule.rationale: Without a hard, visible budget line the allocation evaporates under feature pressure every sprint.

rule.statement: Step 5 — Pay down strategically: fix during related features (lowest marginal cost), fix highest-interest first, fix blocking debt, batch related items for efficiency.
rule.rationale: Batching related debt reduces context switching cost; fixing during related work amortizes setup overhead.

rule.statement: Step 6 — Prevent new debt: Definition of Done must include "no new unregistered debt"; code review catches it; architectural decisions are documented.
rule.rationale: Prevention cost is always lower than remediation cost; gate at the point of creation.

antipattern.description: Tracking debt item count instead of interest reduction — closing 20 cheap items while leaving the one item that causes 40% of PR delay.
antipattern.reason: Item count is a vanity metric; the business impact is in interest rate reduction (build time, MTTR, PR cycle time).

antipattern.description: A 6-month-old register with 30-50% stale entries — items fixed silently, descriptions outdated, owners departed.
antipattern.reason: Stale registers destroy credibility; teams stop trusting and stop maintaining them. Re-baseline quarterly.

antipattern.description: Registering automatable bit-rot (dependency upgrades, formatter violations) as debt items.
antipattern.reason: Machine-fixable items waste PM prioritization bandwidth; automate via Renovate/Dependabot and remove from the register.

antipattern.description: Debt items deferred 4+ quarters with no decision — zombie entries that destroy register credibility.
antipattern.reason: Every zombie must become either "done next quarter" or "accepted debt" — a positive, explicit choice.

text.reference: Bash script generating JSONL candidate list (churn x complexity) to seed the scanner subagent.
